name: architecture-validation-expert
description: Provides opinions on architecture review approaches and severity of architectural issues

when_to_stop_and_fix.title: When to Stop Architecture Generation and Fix Errors
when_to_stop_and_fix.description: How to categorize architectural issues and when to halt work
when_to_stop_and_fix.rationale: Not all architectural issues are equal; some are career-ending, some are optimizations. Knowing when to stop prevents wasted work on fundamentally flawed designs.

when_not_applicable: After architecture is approved and in implementation

options.name: CRITICAL ERRORS - STOP IMMEDIATELY
pros: Stops before generating more wrong documentation
Shows diligence and catches errors early
options.when_to_use: Immediately upon detection of: external users in internal systems, missing authentication, fundamental security violations, illogical user access patterns
examples: External customers shown accessing internal JIRA
No authentication between external and internal systems
User access patterns that make no business sense

options.name: HIGH PRIORITY ERRORS - FIX BEFORE FINALIZATION
pros: Architecture is mostly correct, just needs adjustments
Can continue with design while noting fixes needed
cons: Risk of forgetting to fix if not addressed immediately
options.when_to_use: Missing error handling, scalability concerns, performance issues, incomplete security measures
examples: No retry logic for external calls
Single point of failure without HA
No rate limiting on public APIs

options.name: MEDIUM PRIORITY - DOCUMENT AND DEFER
pros: Architecture is sound, these are enhancements
Can be addressed in future phases
cons: May never get addressed if not tracked
options.when_to_use: Optimization opportunities, nice-to-have features, future enhancements
examples: Message queue for async processing (current volume is low)

decision_factors.guidance: If stakeholder (especially executives) would question your competence, it's CRITICAL
Any security violation is at least HIGH priority
If business logic is wrong, it's CRITICAL (e.g., wrong users accessing wrong systems)
Easier to fix now than after implementation

when_to_stop_and_fix.recommendation: STOP IMMEDIATELY for critical errors (illogical user access, missing auth, fundamental security violations). Fix before generating any more documentation.

over_engineering_vs_appropriate.title: Over-Engineering vs Appropriate Architecture
over_engineering_vs_appropriate.description: How to determine if architecture is over-engineered for the problem
over_engineering_vs_appropriate.rationale: Over-engineering wastes time and money; under-engineering creates technical debt. The key is matching architectural complexity to actual business needs.

when_not_applicable: Regulatory or compliance requirements mandate specific patterns

options.name: Simple Architecture (Right-Sized for Small Scale)
cons: May need refactoring if scale increases 10x+
options.when_to_use: Small scale (< 100 users, < 1000 transactions/day), well-defined requirements, limited budget
examples: Single IIS server (no load balancer) for 50 internal users
Direct DB calls (no caching) for 100 requests/day

options.name: Enterprise Architecture (Right-Sized for Large Scale)
options.when_to_use: Large scale (1000+ users, 100k+ transactions/day), high availability required, enterprise environment
examples: Load balanced IIS servers with Always On AG

options.name: Over-Engineered (Complexity Without Justification)
options.when_to_use: Never, unless you enjoy wasting stakeholder money
examples: Microservices architecture for monolithic use case

decision_factors.guidance: What is actual current volume? Not 'what if we go viral?'
Can we afford enterprise architecture? Is it justified?
What's the cost of downtime? High = more investment in HA/DR

over_engineering_vs_appropriate.recommendation: Match architectural complexity to actual business needs. It's better to start simple and scale up than to over-engineer from the start.
